status_notes: Initial research from AWS Secrets Manager and ACM documentation, 2026-04-04. Pass 1 (service docs). Needs Pass 2 (API), Pass 3 (cross-reference), and Pass 5 (human review with live AWS environment).
official_name: AWS Secrets Manager and Certificate Manager
notes: Secrets management with automatic rotation, and TLS certificate provisioning.

data.description: Store and manage secrets such as database passwords, API keys, OAuth tokens, and other sensitive credentials. Secrets are encrypted at rest using AWS KMS (default aws/secretsmanager key or customer-managed CMK). Supports text secrets (key-value pairs, JSON) and binary secrets (up to 64 KB). Secrets can be tagged, described, and organized with resource policies for cross-account access.

default.note: Default encryption uses the aws/secretsmanager KMS key. Customer-managed KMS keys can be specified for additional control.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use Secrets Manager instead of hardcoded credentials. Use customer-managed KMS keys for secrets requiring key rotation control or cross-account access. Apply resource policies for least-privilege access.

data.description: Automatic rotation of secrets on a configurable schedule using Lambda functions. Built-in rotation support for Amazon RDS (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB), Amazon Redshift, Amazon DocumentDB, and other services. Custom rotation Lambda functions can be created for any secret type. Rotation uses a multi-user or single-user strategy to avoid downtime.

options.description: Update the password for a single database user. Brief period where old password is invalid.
Alternate between two database users. No downtime — one user is always active while the other is being rotated.
AWS-provided rotation Lambda functions for RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB.
Your own Lambda function implementing the rotation steps (createSecret, setSecret, testSecret, finishSecret).

data.vendor_recommendation: Enable automatic rotation for all database credentials. Use alternating users strategy for production databases to avoid downtime. Set rotation schedules based on your security policy (30-90 days typical).

audit.console: AWS Console → Secrets Manager → {secret} → Rotation configuration

match_nodes.description: Share secrets across AWS accounts via resource policy

data.description: Share secrets with IAM principals in other AWS accounts using resource-based policies. The resource policy on the secret grants the external account permission to access the secret. The external account must also have IAM policies allowing the action. If the secret is encrypted with a customer-managed KMS key, the KMS key policy must also grant cross-account access.
data.vendor_recommendation: Use resource policies for cross-account secret sharing. Prefer customer-managed KMS keys for cross-account scenarios. Limit access to specific principals rather than entire accounts.

audit.console: AWS Console → Secrets Manager → {secret} → Resource permissions

data.description: Automatic versioning of secret values. Each version has a unique version ID and staging labels. Three standard staging labels: AWSCURRENT (active version used by applications), AWSPREVIOUS (previous active version for rollback), and AWSPENDING (new version being rotated). Applications retrieve AWSCURRENT by default. Supports custom staging labels for additional workflow control.

options.label: AWSCURRENT
options.description: The active version. Default when no version is specified.

options.label: AWSPREVIOUS
options.description: The previously active version. Useful for rollback.

options.label: AWSPENDING
options.description: The version being created during rotation. Promoted to AWSCURRENT after successful rotation.

match_nodes.description: Free public TLS certificates for AWS services

data.description: Provision, manage, and deploy free public SSL/TLS certificates for use with AWS services. Certificates are automatically renewed before expiration. Supported services: Elastic Load Balancing, Amazon CloudFront, Amazon API Gateway, AWS App Runner, and more. Validation via DNS (recommended) or email. Cannot be exported — used only with integrated AWS services.

options.description: Add a CNAME record to prove domain ownership. Recommended — supports automatic renewal without manual intervention.
Email sent to domain registrant contacts. Requires manual approval for renewal.

default.value: Free for use with AWS services
default.note: No charge for public certificates. Private certificates via ACM Private CA have separate pricing.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use DNS validation for automatic renewal. Request certificates in the region where they will be used (us-east-1 for CloudFront). Use wildcard certificates to reduce management overhead.

match_nodes.description: Private Certificate Authority for internal PKI

data.description: AWS Private Certificate Authority (Private CA) for issuing private certificates for internal services, devices, and applications. Supports hierarchical CAs (root and subordinate). Issue certificates for TLS, code signing, email, and custom use cases. Certificates can be used with any service — not limited to AWS. Two modes: General-purpose (long-lived CAs) and Short-lived certificate mode (reduced cost, 7-day max validity).

options.description: Standard CA for issuing certificates with any validity period. Supports CRL and OCSP.
Reduced pricing. Certificates valid for max 7 days. No revocation support needed.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use Private CA for internal service-to-service TLS (mTLS). Use short-lived certificate mode for ephemeral workloads. Implement a CA hierarchy with offline root CA and online subordinate CAs.

audit.console: AWS Console → Private CA → Private certificate authorities

match_nodes.description: Public certificates logged to Certificate Transparency logs

data.description: All public certificates issued by ACM are logged to Certificate Transparency (CT) logs by default, as required by major browsers. CT logging makes certificate issuance publicly auditable. Organizations can opt out of CT logging for specific certificates, though this may cause browser warnings.

default.value: Enabled by default for all public certificates
default.note: CT logging is the default. Opting out requires explicit action and is not recommended for public-facing certificates.

data.vendor_recommendation: Keep CT logging enabled for all public certificates. Use CT log monitoring to detect unauthorized certificate issuance for your domains.

audit.note: Check CT logs at https://crt.sh/ or https://transparencyreport.google.com/https/certificates

configure.note: Opting out is not recommended for production public certificates.
